# AI for Industry Challenge (AIC) Toolkit — Deep Reference

> A complete simulation-to-evaluation platform for dexterous cable-insertion robotics, where participants submit a single Python `Policy` class and the harness handles all ROS 2 lifecycle, impedance control, sensor fusion, and scoring.

**Generated by**: repo-deep-dive workflow
**Date**: 2026-04-22
**Depth**: deep

---

## Quick Start

```bash
# 1. Clone and install Python/ROS dependencies via Pixi
git clone https://github.com/intrinsic-dev/aic.git && cd aic
pixi install

# 2. Pull and start the evaluation container (requires Docker)
export DBX_CONTAINER_MANAGER=docker
docker pull ghcr.io/intrinsic-dev/aic/aic_eval:latest
distrobox create -r --nvidia -i ghcr.io/intrinsic-dev/aic/aic_eval:latest aic_eval
distrobox enter -r aic_eval -- /entrypoint.sh ground_truth:=false start_aic_engine:=true

# 3. Run an example policy from the host (separate terminal)
pixi run ros2 run aic_model aic_model \
  --ros-args -p use_sim_time:=true \
             -p policy:=aic_example_policies.ros.WaveArm

# 4. (Optional) Run the full stack via Docker Compose instead
docker compose -f docker/docker-compose.yaml up
```

Results land in `$HOME/aic_results/` (override with `$AIC_RESULTS_DIR`).

---

## Key Concepts

**Policy** (`aic_model/aic_model/policy.py:70`)
The abstract base class every participant must subclass. It defines a single method — `insert_cable(task, get_observation, move_robot, send_feedback)` — which runs in a background thread once per task. All ROS 2 boilerplate (lifecycle, pub/sub, action server) is handled by the framework. See [API Reference → SDK](api-reference.md#7-sdk--library-api-python).

**ROS 2 Lifecycle Node Contract** (`aic_model/aic_model/aic_model.py:53`)
The participant model must expose a ROS 2 LifecycleNode named `aic_model`. The engine drives it through `Unconfigured → Configured → Active → Inactive → Cleaned` and actively validates that goals are rejected before activation and accepted after. Deviations cause an infrastructure-level failure, not a task failure. See [Architecture → Key Abstractions](architecture.md#key-abstractions).

**Observation** (`aic_interfaces/aic_model_interfaces/msg/Observation.msg`)
The sole sensor bundle the policy legally sees: three wrist-camera images + camera infos, a 6-DOF force/torque reading, joint states (7 joints, normalized order), and a `ControllerState` with real-time TCP pose and tracking error. Published by `aic_adapter` at ~20 Hz. See [API Reference → Message Types](api-reference.md#4-message-type-reference).

**Impedance Control** (`aic_controller/src/aic_controller.cpp`)
The robot is driven by a 500 Hz Cartesian or joint impedance controller. Policies send sparse 10–30 Hz targets (`MotionUpdate` or `JointMotionUpdate`); the controller interpolates and computes torques. Stiffness and damping are tunable per command to express soft compliance for insertion or stiff tracking for free-space motion. See [API Reference → Controller Config](api-reference.md#13-controller-configuration-parameters).

**Trial / Task** (`aic_engine/config/sample_config.yaml`)
All scene geometry (task board pose, cable placement, mount layout, per-task time limits) is declarative YAML, parsed at engine startup. A *Trial* groups a scene with an ordered list of *Tasks*; each Task carries cable/plug/port identifiers and a `time_limit` (sim-clock seconds). See [Integration Guide → Engine YAML](integration-guide.md#22-aic_engine-yaml-configuration).

**Zenoh RMW**
The official evaluation transport. The eval container runs a Zenoh router on TCP 7447; the model container connects as a peer. Standard DDS will not cross the Docker network boundary. Each parallel evaluation instance requires its own Compose project name (for network isolation) and, if using host-network mode, its own router port. See [Integration Guide → Parallel Deployment](integration-guide.md#4-parallel-deployment).

**Two-Container Deployment**
The evaluation stack (`aic_engine`, `aic_controller`, `aic_adapter`, Gazebo) lives in an immutable `aic_eval` container managed by organizers. Participants submit a `aic_model` container image. They communicate exclusively via ROS 2 over the Zenoh bridge. The eval container exits with code 0 when scoring completes (score in `$AIC_RESULTS_DIR/score.yaml`); parse the YAML rather than relying on the exit code. See [Integration Guide → CLI Subprocess Pattern](integration-guide.md#11-as-a-cli-subprocess).

## Cross-Cutting Notes

### Inconsistencies and Gaps

- **`observations` vs `/observations` topic name** — The API Reference (§1.1) and the Architecture doc refer to the observation topic as `observations` (no leading slash), while the Overview uses `/aic_adapter/observation`. The authoritative source is `aic_model/aic_model/aic_model.py:~107` (`observations` without namespace prefix). Verify the actual published topic name before writing subscribers.

- **`policy` parameter format** — The API Reference (§7.5) says the `policy` parameter is a fully-qualified module + class path (e.g. `my_package.ros.MyPolicy`) and the last component becomes the class name. The Integration Guide (§1.4) shows two separate parameters `policy_module` and `policy_class`. Cross-check `aic_model/aic_model/aic_model.py:56–79` to determine the exact current behavior before configuring your launch file.

- **Scoring tier 3 location** — The Integration Guide (§2.3) references a `ScoringTier3` node for task success, but the Architecture and Overview docs describe Tier 3 as being evaluated from Gazebo `CablePlugin` / `ScoringPlugin` events, not a separate standalone node. The source of truth is `aic_scoring/src/`.

- **Observation rate** — The Overview states ~20 Hz; the API Reference states 20 Hz; the Architecture diagram shows the adapter fires on camera frames at ~30 Hz. Actual observation rate is camera-frame-gated and may vary under load.

- **`policy` default value** — The API Reference (§7.5) shows the default as `"WaveArm"`, while the CLI section (§8) shows `"aic_example_policies.ros.WaveArm"`. Use the fully-qualified form.

### Things New Integrators Must Know

1. **Move heavy imports into `__init__`, not module scope.** Top-level `import torch` runs during the 30-second model discovery window. If it exceeds the window, the engine aborts before your node is even registered. See [Developer Guide → Failure Mode 2 & 6](developer-guide.md#common-failure-modes).

2. **Use sim-clock for all timing.** `task.time_limit` is enforced against simulation time, not wall time. `time.sleep()` and `time.time()` diverge from sim time, especially on cloud hardware. Use `policy.sleep_for()` and `policy.time_now()`.

3. **The engine validates lifecycle rejection.** A goal sent to a `configured` (not yet `active`) model must be rejected. If your custom node incorrectly accepts it, the entire trial is aborted at the `ModelReady` stage.

4. **Each parallel run needs its own `COMPOSE_PROJECT_NAME` and results directory.** Without a distinct project name, Docker networks and container names collide. Without a distinct results directory, `score.yaml` from concurrent runs overwrite each other.

5. **`AIC_RESULTS_DIR` parent must pre-exist.** The engine does not create parent directories. A missing parent causes the engine to error at startup, not at trial time.

6. **Camera timestamp sync tolerance is 1 ms.** Under CPU load, Gazebo camera plugins can drift apart; the adapter silently drops frames and `get_observation()` returns stale data indefinitely. Monitor timestamp progression in your policy loop.

7. **Bag files grow large.** 1–5 GB per trial is typical. For dashboards running many concurrent evaluations, implement a cleanup policy for `$AIC_RESULTS_DIR`.

### Sections Thin on Source Material

- `aic_isaac` is documented only by a `README.md` stub; integration details are incomplete.
- Tier 3 scoring (insertion precision, partial-insertion thresholds) is not fully documented in either the API Reference or the scoring package; the `ScoringPlugin` protobuf (`aic_gazebo/proto/scoring.proto`) is the primary source.
- CI workflow names (`build.yml`, `pixi.yml`, `style.yml`) are mentioned in the Developer Guide but the workflow files were not deeply inspected; treat CI details as approximate.
